summary.rationale: Positive employee sentiment; high innovation culture; 40 reviews

reviews.title: Great company to work for
reviews.pros: Great work life balance, great team
reviews.cons: No bad reviews, liked everything about working there

reviews.title: Thoughts after 10 years....
reviews.pros:
1.  If you love tech, this is a great place.  No doubt you'll talk tech (mostly the MSFT stack) from enterprise to consumer - from PCs to phones to Xboxes - from datacenter to desktop.

2.  What were GREAT benefits are now VERY GOOD (took a small step down) but still probably better than you'll find at 99% of large corporations.  If you've got family - the value of the benefits is even higher.  401k match is nice.

3.  Even with it's struggles MSFT is still a cash printing machine.  This means if you can keep your nose clean and do reasonable work, you can have a stable job, pay your bills, feed your family, and not worry (too much) about layoffs.  The stock you own likely won't tank, but probably won't go up much either.  You'll get a bonus each year and some stock.  It's a decent life if you aren't looking to light the world on fire.
reviews.cons:
Brand on Your Resume:  After many years of losing market share and struggling to be at the front end of innovation and the fact that there's 90,000 employees, don't think MSFT is necessarily going to be attractive on your resume to more agile and smaller companies.

Managing Your Career:  Make you say this out loud so it registers - 90,000 employees work there.  Double that for vendors.  It is VERY hard to "stand out" and move up in the company.  Don't expect your manager to be much of an advocate or enabler to help you meet your career goals - they are basically trying to survive the stack rank every year too.  Not familiar with the stack rank?  Check out the 2012 Vanity Fair article called "Microsoft's Lost Decade".
reviews.advice:
I'll type it here - but I don't they are listening...
1.  Help proven talent move laterally in the company for new opportunities.
2.  Kill/evolve the stack rank.  Here's basically how it's viewed:.  30% of the company gets 1s and 2s - and they are happy.  50% get 3s and that basically feels like getting a "C" in school.  This sucks for talented people when a multitude of UNCONTROLLABLE conditions keep you from getting into 1 or 2 range.  The 20% getting anything below a 3 are walking dead.  In summary, 70% of the company walk away from the review cycle feeling like crap.  This is no good.

After 10 years, I'm leaving - it's just a matter of time until I find the right opportunity.  I need to find a company spends 90% of it's time building technology experiences, as opposed to 90% of its time building PowerPoints.  And I expect to refuse the exit interview - because if you really cared what I thought, you wouldn't ask me after I decided to leave.

reviews.title: A changed company culture...
reviews.pros: Good at first glance. Was once firmly grounded in a culture of customer empathy, and caring for employees.
reviews.cons:
My experience at Microsoft reflected a highly metrics-driven culture, with a strong emphasis on meeting growth targets and satisfying shareholder expectations. In that environment, enterprise customers can at times feel prioritized primarily based on their level of spend or strategic commitment, rather than as long-term partners.

From a sales perspective, there is significant focus on securing large cloud consumption agreements. While these deals are critical to the company’s strategy, the internal incentive structures do not always align with the effort required to land them, which can create tension within account teams. In some cases, this dynamic can also impact broader account strategy, particularly when short-term targets take precedence over long-term relationship development.

Compensation and rewards are often structured with a meaningful reliance on stock-based incentives. While this can be attractive, it also introduces risk, particularly during periods of cost optimization, where organizational changes may affect roles regardless of individual performance or track record.

Overall, Microsoft offers strong technology, scale, and opportunity. However, prospective employees should be aware of the performance intensity, the importance of internal alignment on incentives, and the evolving balance between customer-centricity and growth execution.
reviews.advice: You work hard for a company, I don't think Microsoft cares anymore. It certainly doesn't pay top dollar, it relies too heavily on stock.

reviews.title: Pay sucks, but opportunities are huge
reviews.pros: It's great to be at the forefront of technology and have major companies around the world create products just because you asked for it. It's also great to build solutions that the world runs on, so the scope is huge.
reviews.cons: Doesn't give one iota about consumers anymore. Focus is on demo worthy stuff - forget about bug debt or doing what's right for the customer. Keep seeing people fired every week - even successful people - so you can never feel safe in your job anymore.
reviews.advice: Washington State is unbearable to live in because of the cost of living, so the terrible pay will catch up to in 2026 if you don't do something. I know you want AI to replace us all, but you are screwed if you get a brain drain and the beneficiaries will be your competitors. Your ROI on training us all will be < $0.

reviews.title: Great place to work overall, but team dependent
reviews.pros:
Benefits are really great, healthcare doesn't get deducted from paychecks.
Unlimited AI coding tools budget.
Smart and helpful coworkers.
Great work life balance and benefits for families.
Lots of opportunity for internal movement to different areas.
reviews.cons:
Compensation isn't as high as other big tech companies.
Stock price is down 25% YTD.
Expected to own and debug systems you have never worked with during on call shifts.
Slow promotion velocity, mostly depends on years in role in my experience.
reviews.advice: AI is being forced into some places that don't benefit from it. The tech is revolutionary, but there needs to be more space for discourse about where it is not a good fit, without being made to feel like your an AI hater or out of date.

reviews.title: Morale Is Nonexistent & Clique Culture Runs Deep
reviews.pros:
•Brand recognition
•Some talented, hardworking individuals (primarily vendors) who genuinely care about delivering results
•Remote work options depending on role
reviews.cons:
•Pay has dropped significantly while expectations have increased, including routine unpaid overtime that is treated as the norm rather than the exception.
•Vendors perform the majority of hands-on work, while many FTEs function primarily as managers, reviewers, or overseers rather than contributors. Many FTEs are running full-time businesses while working. Leadership asleep at the wheel.
•Morale is extremely low. Burnout is widespread, and talent is treated as disposable rather than valued. Advancement is dead.
•The workplace is clique-driven and political. Advancement and protection depend more on alignment and relationships than performance.
•Despite public messaging, there is little tolerance for differing viewpoints. If you voice an opinion that doesn’t align with the dominant culture, you risk being sidelined or targeted.
•Poor vendor management: abrupt terminations, lack of notice, no handoff, and zero concern for operational continuity.
•Heavy cost-cutting through external contracting has undermined quality, strained remaining talent, and created instability.
•Quality consulting firms are undercut by low-cost contracting models, resulting in reduced standards and increased pressure on desperate workers navigating layoffs.
reviews.advice:
Open your eyes and engage with what is actually happening on the ground. Attend meetings, listen in on working sessions, and occasionally drop into events and presentations without advance notice. Stop surrounding yourselves with “yes” people and filtered narratives.

Vendors are doing the bulk of the execution. Acknowledge it, compensate fairly, and treat them as strategic partners—not expendable labor—or the organization will continue to erode delivery quality and trust.

Address the normalization of unpaid overtime, rebuild psychological safety, and dismantle clique-driven leadership dynamics. A culture that punishes dissent and exploits overwork will continue to lose its strongest talent.

reviews.title: Software Engineer Intern
reviews.pros:
Interning at Google's California branch was honestly one of the best decisions I've made early in my career. From day one, the team made me feel like a real contributor rather than just an intern passing through. My mentor was genuinely invested in my growth — always available to answer questions, provide code feedback, and help me navigate the codebase without making me feel like a burden.

The engineering culture here is collaborative in a way that's hard to put into words. People are sharp, but they're also generous with their knowledge. I never felt intimidated asking for help, which made learning so much faster.
reviews.cons: Work-life balance can be a challenge. The pace is fast and expectations are high, which is exciting but can feel overwhelming at times. Boundaries are important here — you have to be intentional about logging off.
reviews.advice: Invest in helping interns find balance early. The energy and pace at Google is incredible, but it can be easy to over-extend yourself when you're eager to impress. A brief onboarding conversation around sustainable work habits and setting boundaries would go a long way — especially for interns who are new to the industry and may not yet know how to pace themselves in a high-performance environment.

reviews.title: Steady work and interesting team and lab area
reviews.pros:
It's one of the few places with the capabilities for complex physical prototyping at scale.
Access to Microsoft Resources — Full perks of Microsoft employment: excellent compensation,  comprehensive benefits, health programs learning opportunities, and potential for career growth within a massive tech company.
Collaborative, Skilled Environment  Work with talented engineers, makers, and cross-functional teams. Redmond's campus fosters innovation with proximity to Microsoft Research and other labs.
Impactful Projects — Contribute to real products or historical recreations/experiments, offering a sense of accomplishment in a creative, hands-on role.
Location Perks offers a high quality of life outdoor access, tech hub vibe
reviews.cons:
It's an internal facility, so external recognition or resume might be lower compared to core software/research roles at Microsoft. Projects are NDA-heavy or classified.
Bureaucracy and Corporate Structure, you may deal with slow decision-making, shifting priorities, or unclear roles at times seen in some broader Microsoft reviews.
reviews.advice: More opportunity to learn new skills on variable task assignments, less beurocracy, more innovative projects to work on outside of consumer electronics.

reviews.title: Buys companies just to eliminate the staff and outsource US jobs to India
reviews.pros: Company resources for employees (while I had them, the company gave me an email address, had me complete trainings, and explore their virtual desktop and company network just to turn around and lay off myself alongside most of my coworkers so they could outsource my job to India)
reviews.cons: CEO Satya Nadella is from India. After Microsoft purchased Nuance Communications (company I was working for), Satya immediately established plans to outsource our virtual AI DAX scribe jobs to INDIA. He is obviously out to cushion his own country and doesn't care about onboarding employees from the companies Microsoft keeps buying
reviews.advice: Yeah, I was really happy with my job and honored to become a Microsoft employee, only to have it yanked from my hand. I got a Nuance + Microsoft Better Together welcome box with gifts in it...welcoming me to a company that had ZERO intentions on keeping me or my coworkers as employees. It's dirty, misleading, and down right pure crap to do that to people. We need jobs IN OUR COUNTRY FOR US. I can't stand it when companies want to US citizens to BUY their products but REFUSE to employ and pay US citizens to MAKE THOSE SAME PRODUCTS. MICROSOFT LOST ALL RESPECT OF OVER 10,000 PEOPLE PULLING THAT SHADY NONSENSE

reviews.title: Great pay, but I’m basically a janitor in Bangalore 2.0
reviews.pros: The pros are decent. The pay is actually solid for garbage work, the benefits are ridiculous, and the trash bags are top-tier, thick, lavender scented, basically executive garbage. I get all the free Red Bull I can stomach from the micro kitchens I empty, and job security is bulletproof. With the amount of waste these employees generate, I’ll have a job long after the AI apocalypse.
reviews.cons: But the cons are overwhelming. The “smart” trash compactors are smarter than half the product managers yet still jam every Tuesday because someone tried shoving a 27" monitor in there. Leadership loves preaching sustainability initiatives while ordering 400 catered lunches a day that get immediately thrown away. I’m not collecting garbage anymore, I’m hauling performative ESG theater in Hefty bags. And don’t even get me started on the smell near Building 99 after a big Windows release. It’s spiritual.
reviews.advice:
I’ve been a garbage collector at Microsoft’s Redmond campus for two years now, and let me tell you, this place isn’t a tech headquarters anymore. It’s India with better WiFi and worse drainage. Every single morning I roll up and it looks like someone air-dropped a Mumbai street market straight into the Pacific Northwest. The ground is littered with cardboard curry containers, half-eaten dosas, banana peels, and enough discarded disposable chai cups to build a second campus. The seagulls have unionized and started demanding stock options.

My advice to management is simple: stop pretending this campus is some pristine futuristic utopia while I’m out here playing real life Papers, Please with rotting paneer tikka masala at 6 a.m. If you’re going to keep hiring thousands of new people every quarter, maybe invest in bigger bins instead of more PowerPoint decks. Or here’s a radical idea, eat the food you order for once.

reviews.title: good benefits and good people
reviews.pros: The health benefits were great and we received shares of stock quarterly as well as had the opportunity to buy Microsoft stock discounted. We also got a health stipend to spend on health and wellness or remote office supplies. The people who work there are great and are some of the smartest most capable people I've ever met.
reviews.cons: I was laid off along with 10,000 people and was given a very minimal lump sum payout and only was given 21 of my 95 shares of unvested stock. At the end of the day it is a huge company and you are only a number to them.

reviews.title: An endless AI cycle at a company that is becoming increasingly mocked everywhere
reviews.pros:
• Many opportunities for cross pollination; learning and sharing skills with others
• Lots of great teammates and talented individuals to bump elbows with
reviews.cons:
• Very difficult to move vertically up the company ladder, feels like a club
• Salaries are falling below industry standard
• Despite public outlook, AI is still #1 priority for senior leadership, and every project is centered around how to further implement into product
• RTO policy betrays company's earlier promises, and overall seems like a means to force resignations to save face and keep company from paying out severance
reviews.advice: Truly listen to customers rather than just saying you are listening to customers. There are avenues to return Microsoft back into a favorable light, but that will require us to swallow our pride and admit mistakes. We used to be good at doing that in the past, but now it seems like we are so concerned about being pioneers for AI that we have become pioneers in corporate greed and selective hearing.

reviews.title: Not worth it
reviews.pros: The people here are still great for the most part, and there are good benefits in terms of health care, insurance, etc.
reviews.cons: The pay has never been good, but the culture and work life balance used to make up for it. Now, the culture is falling apart due to increased pressure, cuts to funding for morale and ERGs, and layoffs. The pressure to ship quickly is high, but the barriers to get things done are only getting higher and higher. Leadership still hasn't figured out a solid direction in terms of AI, so priorities are constantly changing and re-orgs happen almost monthly.
reviews.advice:
Figure out company direction so priorities aren't constantly changing
Reduce the overhead required to ship before increasing pressure to do it quickly
Culture still matters, and burned out employees don't do a good job because they don't care

reviews.title: Best Company I've worked for
reviews.pros:
Strong compensation & benefits – Competitive salary, bonuses, stock options, and solid healthcare.
Brand & resume value – A globally respected name that can open doors later.
Inclusive culture – Emphasis on collaboration, diversity, and a “growth mindset” culture.
reviews.cons:
Bureaucracy – Large organization means slower decision-making and more layers of approval.
Pace varies by team – Some teams move slowly, which can be frustrating if you prefer fast execution.

reviews.title: Strong Growth Environment with High Expectations
reviews.pros: Collaborative team culture with strong technical expertise. Clear exposure to large strategic accounts and cross-functional engagement. Leadership is accessible and performance driven. Opportunities to develop both commercial and strategic skills in a competitive market. Good visibility into long-term customer programs.
reviews.cons: High performance expectations with limited internal resources at times. Cross-regional coordination can slow decision making. Forecast accuracy and demand volatility create pressure on account teams. Internal processes occasionally lack alignment across departments.
reviews.advice: Continue investing in clearer cross-functional alignment and faster internal decision cycles. Strengthen resource support for strategic accounts to balance workload and growth objectives. Improve transparency around long-term roadmap priorities so teams can proactively align customer engagement strategies.

reviews.title: Great Company but the Work Culture is Shifting
reviews.pros:
Working with some of the most talented and driven individuals in the industry is amazing.
Clarity of direction in the business is communicated well.
There are some truly great managers that support your work endeavors.
reviews.cons:
Career progression past a certain point for ICs becomes more about shameless self-promotion and signaling.
The drive to improve margins to accommodate AI investments is slowly mutating the culture. 
Employees feel increasingly disposable and work life balance is suffering,
reviews.advice:
Ease up on the singular focus with AI and shore up our other areas of business to reduce risk. The downsides of that risk will tank stock prices with most of the eggs in that one basket.

"Do more with less" with record profits and the ever looming threat of layoffs is a dark paradox. Everyone I know is grinding so hard but that might be irrelevant to their continued employment.

reviews.title: Extremely varied experience depending on team, but good Work-Life Balance overall
reviews.pros: Better Work-Life balance than other Big Tech companies; Hybrid Work for major hubs, Remote Work for some; Cool coworkers depending on Org; Investment in Employee Growth; Possibility for Internal movement, laterally and vertically; Some Employees tend to stay for a very long time (that says something); Diverse & Inclusive environment (at least in my Org)
reviews.cons: Work and Experience are HUGELY dependent on the Org/ Product and Manager; Primary focus on AI and not much else over the past few years; Return to Office for certain areas despite past reassurances to not touch Remote work; Worse Total Compensation than other Big Tech companies; non-main campuses are a bit lackluster compared to other companies'; Lack of Communication between Products, Teams, and Orgs
reviews.advice: Focus more on getting down the fundamentals. A lot of our core experiences are broken or outdated, but we continue to push for new features

reviews.title: Great company but your team is the company so choose team carefully
reviews.pros: great compensation for work life balance
reviews.cons:
some managers are so bad
very slow to get promoted

reviews.title: Good Benefits
reviews.pros: Products are easy to sell once mature. The name Microsoft opens doors. Great company to work for if you understand how Corporate America works.
reviews.cons: Stack rank is still very much alive. Virtue signaling can be a little much at times. Who your manager is matters quite a bit. GM/VP Leadership turnover is very high. Ivory tower in Redmond at times.

reviews.title: amazing company
reviews.pros: Excellent benefits, great work-life balance, and opportunities to work on world-scale infrastructure.
reviews.cons: I dont see any cons here

reviews.title: not the old microsoft
reviews.pros: Microsoft’s performance review process gives a clear, structured way to talk about impact. It typically encourages you to tie your work to measurable outcomes (delivery, quality, customer value, collaboration), which makes accomplishments easier to explain and helps managers compare contributions consistently across a team. When done well, it also creates a regular rhythm for feedback and growth—so you’re not surprised at year-end—and can help you align on priorities, scope, and career development steps.
reviews.cons: The downside is that the experience can feel inconsistent depending on the org, manager, and calibration dynamics. Even with guidelines, expectations may be interpreted differently across teams, and outcomes can sometimes feel influenced by visibility, narrative-writing skill, or shifting priorities rather than purely day-to-day contribution. The process can also add significant overhead (self-assessments, evidence gathering, review writing), and if feedback isn’t frequent during the year, the review may feel more like a paperwork exercise than a genuinely helpful development tool.

reviews.title: At MAI, work life balance is bad
reviews.pros:
Cool exciting projects to work on
Easy learning opportunities
Great to network with brilliant people
reviews.cons:
The salary is not worth the effort compared to other competitors.
Not a great work life balance sometimes.
other colleagues had to work during their time off.
reviews.advice: Let's try to be unique and not copy other products, that's how we can ultimately stand out, and give salarial compensation for any one who is involved in secret projects and expected to work more hours.

reviews.title: Great opportunity at great company
reviews.pros: Very welcoming and collaborative environment that provided ample opportunity to network with in house attorneys and research novel research questions at the edge of law and technology. Company paid for relocation and housing for the summer. You even complete a performance evaluation that stays in your file in the event you want to apply for an in house job later.
reviews.cons: Not sure if they do the program anymore but hopefully they'll bring it back.

reviews.title: Average Work Experience
reviews.pros:
Good pay
Good Work Life Balance
reviews.cons: Layoffs and fast changing direction

reviews.title: SWE at Microsoft
reviews.pros: Good benefits for health insurance, eye and dental care as well as mental health via spring health platform
reviews.cons: Compensation is somewhat lower relative to some other companies in the tech industry, and the work environment can be quite competitive depending on your team. Career prospects are largely driven by your direct manager and skips budget. AI will likely decide the importance of the work and money you earn.

reviews.title: Mechanical Engineer 2 at Azure hardware
reviews.pros: Chance to work on latest technology, Best team and work life balance. Free medical insurance, good benefits.
reviews.cons: Wrold team , so need to work in asian times as well.

reviews.title: Great place to work but leadership misalignment
reviews.pros:
- Lots of opportunities for growth
- Opportunity to build collaboration skills
- Opportunity to set yourself apart if you work hard
- Good culture
reviews.cons:
- Lots of leadership churn and changing directions
- Competitiveness or misalignment between different teams and orgs
- Pay is less competitive than other big tech companies

reviews.pros: Great company culture and good career growth
reviews.cons: Unclear objectives and leadership vision

reviews.title: Microsoft review
reviews.pros: - Great work life balance
reviews.cons: NO negatives as everything is great

reviews.title: Best
reviews.pros:
easy 
benefits 
good pay
Always busy
reviews.cons:
never have time for family 
to hard

reviews.title: Good
reviews.pros: Good questions  experiences and rewarding
reviews.cons: Nothing to complain about as It was very exciting
reviews.advice: Keep a positive attitude and believe in yourself

reviews.title: Strong engineering culture and learning opportunities
reviews.pros: Great work-life balance, supportive team culture, excellent learning opportunities, strong engineering practices, and access to cutting-edge technologies.
reviews.cons: Large organizational structure can slow decision-making, some internal processes are complex, and project changes can take time to implement.

reviews.title: Boring but pays the bills
reviews.pros:
Good pay
Amazing "work life balance". Mental health breaks, missing meetings for family events, generous time off. If you are getting work done, nobody will be on your case.
Generally nice people and secure job.
reviews.cons:
There's no vision in the company. Most tasks are "a pm wanted to push out a feature in 6 months so he or she could get promoted and move on to a new project." You'll be working on code that's over 30 years old. It's like being an electrician in a preserved 17th century mansion, the asks are simple but implementation is a complex chore trying to navigate such ancient codebases.
80% of engineers just do it because their parents said "engineer or doctor" and they picked engineer, they have no particular passion or competency for the work.

The engineering skills you gain will be specific to microsoft or the product you are working on and will not open up opportunities at other companies. (Anyone need an x++ dev?)
reviews.advice: Microsoft's core competency is compliance and momentum. Most users don't particularly enjoy the software their bosses make them use. You can only ride that train for so long. You need to be actively redesigning the products to make them competitive in the hearts and minds of the public (see Xbox brands moves recently for what not to do).

reviews.title: Great company, amazing people
reviews.pros: Forefront of AI, motivated and driven coworkers
reviews.cons: 4 days a week in office

reviews.title: Great job but toxic culture
reviews.pros: Interesting work and nice people
reviews.cons: Toxic culture and politics matter more than competence. Low pay compared to market

reviews.title: Xbox: Layoff Culture, Chaotic Leadership
reviews.pros: Your coworkers at low levels will be great! Most of the people are awesome, competent, and dependable. Products are fun to work with.
reviews.cons: Xbox has fully transitioned to a layoff culture where no one knows how long they will be employed for or at what point further layoffs will be announced. Many liken in it to a party with friends on the Titanic. Leadership expectations are unrealistic and fully uncommunicated. Many teams and orgs have competing goals. Leadership is stingy with advancement and promotions, often promoting the least deserving (often via nepotism). Over 50% of Xbox's workforce is vendor and contract work, so the majority of workers are underpaid and over tasked. "Do more with less" is standard operational mentality.
reviews.advice: Management doesn't listen to anyone who doesn't have a VP in front of their title. Advice here is wasted.

reviews.title: Get paid tons of money for malicious compliance
reviews.pros: They don’t call us MicroSlop for nothing! The more you use AI to do your work, the more you are rewarded. I now click a few things a few times a day and pretend to care about meetings. This has led to bonuses and raises, all without having to use my brain anymore.
reviews.cons: Our products and services have turned into a dumpster fire. The only way to carry on day by day is to simply not care about the quality of our work. There is also blatant racism: if you’re Indian or Chinese, you’ll be interviewed, hired, and protected from layoffs, no matter how terrible you are. If you’re not, you won’t even get an interview.
reviews.advice: Despite the regularly occurring layoffs, we seem to retain the bad people. We need to fire about 50% of the company and change the way we hire. We need people who actually care about the company. We used to empower people, but now we don’t. Such a shame.

reviews.title: Supportive
reviews.pros: During this internship there was a real motivation to learn and explore Microsoft's products and processes. We were constantly encouraged to add our opinions and input into meetings and make suggestions if we felt there was a better product to use or way to design our code.
reviews.cons: Did not receive a letter of recommendation after asking, and following up.

reviews.title: Bad experience
reviews.pros: good pay, good office premises.
reviews.cons: bad work ethic, corrupt and favouritism practicing seniors

reviews.title: meh
reviews.pros: the food is good and the people are chill
reviews.cons: the work is a bit slow
